name: Arthur Blythe
abstract: Arthur Murray Blythe (July 5, 1940 – March 27, 2017) was an American jazz alto saxophonist and composer. He was described by critic Chris Kelsey as displaying "one of the most easily recognizable alto sax sounds in jazz, big and round, with a fast, wide vibrato and an aggressive, precise manner of phrasing" and furthermore as straddling …

concerts.label: One Night Stand: A Keyboard Concert
JVC Jazz Festival: Steve Reich and Musicians with World Saxophone Quartet

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: Lester called me to do something with him and Arthur Blythe and Claudine Amina Myers in New York... we went in the studio and recorded 'New Directions'
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mike Wofford
about_quotes.evidence: there’s a wonderful alto player in New York I’ve never met but he’s from San Diego named Arthur Blythe.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mike Wofford interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 13, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: the first album I did with our group Special Edition, which had Arthur Blythe... in it
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, April 7, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Mcbee
about_quotes.evidence: we were going to, after the concert, celebrate Kirk Lightsey’s birthday... And so we’re performing a tune by Arthur Blythe that I couldn’t find anything ever to do with that tune because it was well with respected to Arthur, I did not like the tune.

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: And then there’s Benny Golson, Arthur Blythe, Nathan Davis and myself. And so that band... is — because being with Benny Golson and playing those Benny Golson arrangements and things, so it’s really great, so I like doing that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Freeman interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: when David and Black Arthur Blythe leave then Chico Freeman and John Purcell come in... The change of personnel.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
